choices.text: are not able to focus and concentrate sufficiently The Case Studies in this assignment are considered mini-experiments which involve human behaviour. Case Studies A-D are summarized from Mindless Eating by Brian Wansink. Use Case Study A to answer multiple-choice items 12 and 13. Case Study A Billy, a galley cook on a Navy ship, was facing daily complaints from the crew about the lemon Jell-O™ he was serving. Because of an ordering error, lemon was the only flavour of Jell-O purchased for consumption during the four-month exercise. There was no cherry Jell-O™ and the crew was unhappy. Because the ship was not going to be in port (land) for another two months, Billy had to act ina creative manner - he added red food colouring to the lemon Jell-O™. When the red-coloured lemon Jell-O™ was served to the sailors, no one complained.

choices.label: D
choices.text: satisfaction level of the sailors ADLC Personal Psychology 20 3 Assignment Booklet 1 Personal Psychology 20 Use Case Study B to answer multiple-choice items 14 and 15. Case Study B One hundred forty (140) male college students were asked to read a summary of a fictitious date between Brad and Barb. The students were divided into two groups of 70 people each. The summary described going to a movie and gave much detail about what Brad did on the date. The only difference in the descriptions that each group was given related to the amount of popcorn Brad ate during the movie. In one version of the summary Brad ate “a couple of handfuls” of his popcorn. In the other version Brad ate “almost all" of his popcorn. Researchers asked the two groups of men how much weight (in pounds) they thought Brad could bench press. Students who read that Brad ate almost all of his popcorn imagined Brad lifting, on average, 21 more pounds than the Brad who only ate a couple of handfuls of his popcorn.

choices.text: supposition Use Case Study C to answer multiple-choice items 19 and 20. Case Study C A group of students starting university were treated to a movie and given popcorn as part of their university orientation experience. Before the movie began, the students were also offered jelly beans. Half of the students were presented jelly beans in a tray divided into six sections. Each section contained 200 jelly beans of one flavour type. The other half of the students were offered the jelly beans in a mixed container. There were still six flavours, but they were not neatly separated from each other. The researchers then calculated the average number of jelly beans taken by individuals in the two groups. When the jelly beans were presented in the tray with the different flavours separated from each other, students took an average of 12 jelly beans. Students who were offered the disorganized assortment took an average of 23 jelly beans. It appeared that mixing the flavours nearly doubled the number of jelly beans consumed! ADLC Personal Psychology 20 5 Assignment Booklet 1 Personal Psychology 20
